Narrative_Meaning: Sevens signify surprises, whether in the sense of conspiracy, illnesses and light hearted fun.

Yarn.Yarn_Description: The Crystal represents both riches and the fragility of finery. Riches are easily bestowed by the card, but easily taken away again too. The Crystal can represent a change of circumstances, clarity of purpose, or signify a flaw or weakness that is hidden, but very important.

Significator.Plot: The Crystal Plot is about changing circumstances and fortunes. It may appear like a Rags-To-Riches story, but there is more emphasis on realising that money is essentially worthless, and that human connections are more important than money.
Significator.Side: A side marked with the Crystal is getting richer off the events of the Plot, somehow they are making bank off the Conflict, this might be through taxes, sales, services provided or because off a Robin Hood style vigilante, depending upon the genre and era.
Significator.Character:
The Crystal Significator indicates a Character who so rich that money has no meaning to them.

They Gain Yin when they listen to another talk.

They Gain Yang when they give advice, aid or time to another.

They Gain Chi when they give Chi or Descendants to other Characters.

They gain +1 Success Level on all Purchase or summoning Tests and Ordeal actions.
Significator.Scene: The Crystal Scene is a point where a great gain is made by the Characters. This may be the final reward, or it may be the McGuffin or some other important Plot Device, it can even be a Revelation of exactly what the Characters need to do to win. Yarn-Tellers may play this card for either a specific Gain (such as "The Grail is mine!"), or a Specific Revelation (such as "I need to know, how do I kill this immortal?".

Yarn.Aspect_Description: The Hook targets the Character's worst Hitch (usually a Flaw or a Woe) and may trigger it or offer to resolve it as required to Hook the Character.
Yarn.Hook_Description: The Story hooks the by offering to pay them. It could be that this plot is part of their normal job, the next case, or delivery, or it might be that they are specifically offered money to look into (or perhaps turn a blind eye to) something.
Yarn.Fray_Description: The Fray is an attempt to spy upon the enemy, or predict the future somehow, this may involve a trip to a fortune-teller, or running simulations, or use of Wyrd or Yarn abilities. Regardless of method, the fray consists of trying to create a Chronolith of a suitable size to fix a point in the future, or observe the past, or the enemy's current position. This is normally done with a Solitary or Opposed Ordeal of Paradoxical Stakes.
Yarn.Snag_Description: The Flawed Snag can express a few different ways depending upon narrative requirements. The Snag can trigger the PCs Flaws during the Fray (or following), or it can attempt to create a new Flaw. Alternatively, the Snag can indicate that the PC's plan is flawed, and that the result of the Fray will not be what they expected, or wanted.

NPC.Description: Thieves see nothing wrong with taking something shiny of someone else. They usually have an environment that they prefer to operate in or on (highways, crowds, banks, homes, museums, etc), and all those wanted posters would make anyone feel persecuted.

Yarn.Sweeping_Text: This Sweeping grants the PCs a moment of clarity. The Referee or Yarn-Teller should use direct exposition to explain the situation properly to the PC. This can be handled as the PC making this realisation themselves, and a good Yarn-Teller can sometimes arrange this, but if the attempt fails the Yarn-Teller or Ref should be prepared to explain what's really going on. Sometimes this sort of exposition can be handled via Villain monologues, or confessions, but they are not compatible with every genre.
Yarn.History_Description: The Crystal indicates that the history that led to the current situation was a rise of some sort, this may be a rise in wealth, rank, social standing, or something boring like power. Entire empires can rise on the basis of a Crystal History, but more commonly smaller pacts or individuals are usually effected.
Yarn.Situation_Description: The Crystal indicates that the current situation is fragile, delicate or easily broken. This is a situation that is about to change, usually for the worse.
Yarn.Solution_Description: The Crystal indicates that the solution to the current problem is to exploit some flaw in the opposition. This may mean actually directly targeting a Hitch, but more often indicates there is a flaw in their plan, operation, or base that can be exploited somehow.

Revelations.About_Description: The information is about the current situation and plot. It might include information about one side of the Conflict, all the sides and anything in between. It is normal when providing information to always reveal information about the Dominant Side as well as any others.
Revelations.Info_Description: Information about Properties can be as simple as a Location's address, the buildings and treasures that the Embodiment owns, or it can be a list of known properties of the Embodiment or one of its abilities.
Revelations.Vector_Description: Fractures can carry information, with smashed glass telling you how a room was entered, or how fast a car was travelling before it crashed. Fractures can also occur in injuries, which can certainly send a message.
Revelations.Alternate_Description: The information is about how the Embodiment is fragile, they may have some weakness or flaw that might be exploited somehow. A castle wall might have been raised on a section of weaker sandstone, or may have been made with lower quality mortar, and characters always have Hitches that can be revealed.
Revelations.Detail_Description: Some details are clear, they draw attention to themselves. They are the blinking countdown lights on the side of the bomb, the clearly labelled "Drink Me" label, or the glow deep within the flesh of the mutant as it builds its power.

Obstacle.Description: The way ahead is blocked by a window, it may be broken, which will turn it from an Obstacle to a Trap, the trap must then be disarmed. It is possible to break the window and pass the trap simultaneously with a Stunt or Trick.

Tarot.Normal: Target rolls a Complete Failure or +2 Failure Levels.
Tarot.Crossed: +2 Success Levels to one Roll or Ordeal Card.

Ordeal.Stage_Description: The way ahead is through, or around, or on defences of some sort. These might be as simple as a hedge or chain-link fence, or be a gatehouse, or even a fortified citadels walls.
Ordeal.Motional_Description: The lizard men are getting away on a giant Alligator, you'll have to chase them, perhaps on those jet-skis?
Ordeal.Tide_of_Battle_Text: The Flowing forces have formed (or reformed) a defensive line, this might be a wall of shields, a powered defence, a line of armoured vehicles, or a low garden wall. The point is they have something to defend, that offers them a slight advantage (+1 Pips on all Defence cards they play).
Ordeal.Fight_Description:
The way ahead is defended by a Kaiju or Force-of-Nature Monsters, or dozens or hundreds of well armed Cast Extras.
  Facet    Monster  
Burden ⚋ ♣

Suit: Clubs ♣

Ordeal Trump: Defence / Trade / Control / Magic (Negative)

Description: Clubs represent the power of politics and a stoic, blind materialism. Clubs can be seen as a dark corrupting force on the world, crass materialism, sin, magic and anger, but the club is also symbolic of protection, law and justice. In the Tarot the Clubs are called Staves or Wands, in some traditions they represent Intellectual Air, but in most they are Fire.

Tarot Meaning: Power, Fame, Politics, Ability, Money.

Ordeal Text: Ordeal Types: Wealth, Politics, Power. Clubs act as Trump for following Actions: Bartering, Controlling, Corrupting, Emotional-intensity, Guarding, Magic, Displaying might, Politics, Self-Defence, Socializing, and Trade Negotiations. Clubs can be used to defend any type of attack.

Parry:DisarmAll the cards of the attack are immediately discarded, along with the Parry Card. The remaining cards of the Defender's Parry can now each be used to cause the Attacker to drop a Descendant (starting with the attacking weapon usually), can also cut armour straps, belts etc, or steal hats. This basically adds RT equal to the card Pips to the Annex that was used to attack. Every 5 Pips also creates a point of GRT for the Character (representing how much time the Character must spend picking up, or readying the weapon again. Court cards additionally may damage the Descendant that was disarmed, as standard for the Stakes.

Soak: WardThe matched Attack card, and one other randomly selected Attack card are discarded.

Object — The Monster is an object imbued with the semblance of life. Artificial in nature usually, the living object is usually tough and strong. An Object Monster is created with a Monster Pool equal to their Burden Boon. Objects are able increase the Scores of any rolled die and Pips of all played cards by their Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) when appropriate to their form and function. The Object Monster may add Burden Score cards to it’s Monster Pool, if it makes, or has made, modifications, or upgrades to itself, usually using components it has secured. It may add Burden Draw cards to its Monster Pool any time it is mistaken for a normal Object, or is used in accordance to its form or function. The Object Monster must play cards from its Monster Pool to perform any action that is not a part of its normal form and function.
Dominion ⚊ ♣

Suit: Clubs ♣

Ordeal Trump: Defence / Trade / Control / Magic (Negative)

Soak: WardThe matched Attack card, and one other randomly selected Attack card are discarded.

Horde — The Monster is a group or swarm of lesser monsters, it might be an Army, a pack of Bulmäs, a Fortress or a Battle Moon. A Horde is effectively a Pact Descendant. A Horde may pool Values to create larger rolls, however they may never create a Boon greater than their individual Values. Hordes may add Dominion Draw cards to their Monster Pool when the Horde increases in number. The Horde must Play at least one card from their Monster Pool on any action that potentially splits or reduces the Horde (like losing an individual or being attacked). If the Horde Monster Pool is emptied the Horde is scattered as individuals again, and must reform to regain Monster Pool. The Horde automatically adds the current Monster Pool Size (Number of cards) to pay any costs for multiple targets and may also add Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) Pips to any perception-based Actions. E.g. two Vex Extras of Boon 10 (Value 45) that form a Horde become a single Extra of Boon 16 (Value 90), (with probably 1-3 cards in the Monster Pool granting them a bonus to offset any costs from multiple targets and a smaller bonus to perception) but could never create a Boon greater than the individual’s Value(45). If there were 12, or One million, Vexes would give Boon 45 (Value 530) since that is the most powerful their Horde can get.
Fury ⚊ ♣

Suit: Clubs ♣

Ordeal Trump: Defence / Trade / Control / Magic (Negative)

Temperamental — The Monster is the embodiment of an Emotion, usually a negative one. Such Monsters are often capable of supernatural actions, such as telepathy, shape-shifting, or even undying. The Vampire is an embodiment of Lust, with powers of mind control, telepathy and suggestion, as well as undying and shape-shifting abilities. The Werewolf is embodiment of berserk rage, and is almost indestructible unless Silver is used. The Beansidhe is the embodiment of mourning, and able to sense an oncoming death. When they experience or induce their negative emotion the Monster may refill their Monster Pool to Fury Score cards. When affecting a Character who is afflicted with their emotion they may add Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) to their Actions. Temperamentals that are affected by emotional attacks must use cards from their Monster Pool to Ignore (Drawing equal to their Number of Active Hitches Reduced) the effect.
Rook ⚋ ♣

Guardian — The Monster is a Guardian, it guards a certain territory, path, treasure or person. Guardian Monsters may add Rook Draw to their Monster Pool whenever they interfere with another’s attack, or are attacked themselves. The Guardian will add Rook Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever they, or the thing they guard is Wounded or Damaged. A Guardian may spend a card from its Monster Pool to cancel another’s attack, this can be described as a dodge, or a soak. A Guardian may add a Success Level per Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) to any Attack or Defence action. From Smaug to Fafnir dragons are often Guardians, looking after a particular treasure or demanding tributes from a territory. Guardians often use area-denial Splash attacks, such as breath weapons, tail lashes, wing buffeted dust, venom sprays, gaze attacks, and explosions, to drive enemies from their territory.
Sin ⚊ ♣

Suit: Clubs ♣

Ordeal Trump: Defence / Trade / Control / Magic (Negative)

Soak: WardThe matched Attack card, and one other randomly selected Attack card are discarded.

Villain — The Sin Monster is an embodiment of a specific vice, taboo, or sin, often in the form of a human Villain (usually with animalistic features or tendencies). They usually have Annexes and Hitches that reflect their embodied vice or sin, and are often at least partially supernatural. Villains add Sin Draw cards to their Monster Pool when they, or another, indulges in their particular vice or behaviour. Which can be as broad as “Evil” or as focused as “Smokes Gauloises Cigarettes” as you like. Villains may add +1 Score / Pips for each card in their Monster Pool to any action that furthers their diabolical plans. Villains automatically spend Sin Draw Monster Pool cards on creating Obstacles when they escape or are threatened. The genocidal or narcissistic maniac, of the sort British Intelligence seems to tangle with repeatedly, could be a Sin Monster. The ever-hungry cannibal spirit, the Wendigo is definitely an example, as is the Sexual-predatory Lust-embodied Vampire.
Wyrd ⚋ ♣

Suit: Clubs ♣

Ordeal Trump: Defence / Trade / Control / Magic (Negative)

Soak: WardThe matched Attack card, and one other randomly selected Attack card are discarded.

Doom-weaver — The monster is a creature of the Fæ, it can draw and play Yarn cards or Wyrd Tarot cards (as appropriate). The Wyrd Monster may play cards from its Monster Pool as Wyrd Tarot or Yarn cards. The Monster may draw and add Wyrd Draw cards to its Monster Pool whenever it makes a choice, decision, or judgement. The Doom-weaver Monster may draw and Prepare Monster Pool Size Reduced cards whenever anyone else plays Wyrd Tarot or Yarn cards in the same Location. They must play a card from the Monster Pool if they have to undo a choice, decision or judgement they have made. Doom-Weavers come in many forms from the Cait-Sith, the Beansidhe, or even just powerful Mercari, to the Faery nobles of Ironmaster.
  Facet    Monster

Lea.Notes: Small domesticated animals are the most common paths in human occupied lands.

Stress.Description: Stress makes things harder. Things that we could previously bear with grace become impossible to cope with, and all too often the Stress interferes with our skills and talents. Stress can even make us revel in these hardships, justifying and internalising the failures.
Stress.Rules: All Club suited Actions (Burden, Dominion, Fury, Rook, Sin, or Wyrd) add Difficulty equal to the Character’s current Stress in Intrepid Facet Sway. If a penalised Action fails the Character may reduce Stress by 1.

Trauma.Description: Traumas can sometimes lead to a pay-off, this could be an insurance policy paying out, or damages from whoever was responsible, but something happens to give the Character a payment of some sort.
Trauma.Rules: The Trauma immediately Relieves all Stress converting it all into Chi immediately. The Trauma will also pay Chi equal to the Wound the Trauma involves and the Chi cost of any Hitch generated as well.

Age.Description: An Age of Ostentation is occurring. During this age a society becomes obsessed with demonstrations of wealth, power, bravado, pagentry and general excess. The society is rich, but is not humble, spectacles, splendor and splurges are the fashion of the day, as the society swaggers around flaunting its own magnificence. Usually this age is as a result of previous economic success, with little done during the age to prepare for future hard times, the fashionable baubles purchased at great expense now, will be worth nothing when the collapse comes.
Age.Rules: During an Age of Ostentation a society fritters away the wealth it has previously created on fashions, parades, excessive and often questionable celebrations, rather than saving that wealth for leaner times, or reinvesting it to secure the future (although some within the society may do this and be rewarded later for their forward thinking).
